products.title: Pink Flamingo Stylophora
products.body_html:
Pink Flamingo Stylophora: The Highly Saturated Hot Pink Brancher

The Pink Flamingo Stylophora (Stylophora species) is an incredibly vibrant and highly sought-after collector strain of Small Polyp Stony (SPS) coral. Stylophora, often called "Cat's Paw Coral" or "Cluster Coral," is known for being one of the hardiest and fastest-growing SPS genera, often recommended as a great step after Montipora.

This specific strain is named "Pink Flamingo" for its unmistakable, uniform hot pink, fuchsia, or magenta base tissue. The entire colony glows with intense, saturated color. The coral is covered in numerous, small polyps that are typically the same glowing pink color, giving the branches a robust, dense appearance that fluoresces brilliantly under blue-spectrum lighting.

The Pink Flamingo Stylophora grows in a dense, bushy, and blunt-branched morphology, quickly forming robust, rounded colonies. It is considered an Easy to Moderate care SPS coral. It is highly resilient but requires consistent water chemistry, high light, and ample water flow to maintain its rapid growth and brilliant pink saturation.

Diet & Compatibility:

  • Diet: This coral is primarily Photosynthetic (deriving most energy from symbiotic algae, zooxanthellae) but is also an opportunistic Carnivore.
  • Feeding: Supplemental feeding is highly beneficial for accelerating growth and enhancing color saturation. Its small polyps readily capture fine particulate foods. Target feed 2-3 times per week with very fine foods, such as cyclops, rotifers, or commercial coral powders and amino acid solutions.
  • Reef Compatibility: It is Reef Safe and generally Passive. Stylophora lacks powerful sweeper tentacles, but due to its fast and dense branching growth, it can easily shade out and physically irritate adjacent corals. Ensure it has ample space (3-4 inches) from sensitive neighbors.
  • Temperament: Peaceful toward fish and motile invertebrates, and Passive toward other corals. It should be positioned on high rockwork where it receives High PAR lighting (300-450 PAR), which is crucial for maximizing the pink pigment, and strong, chaotic, turbulent water flow to prevent detritus buildup within the dense branches.
  • Water Requirements: Requires stable parameters. The tank should be mature with very low nutrient levels (Nitrate < 5 ppm, Phosphate < 0.1 ppm). Consistency in alkalinity is paramount. Critical parameters: Alkalinity (dKH 8.0-9.5), Calcium (400-450 ppm), and Magnesium (1300-1450 ppm).

Quick Facts

  • Scientific Name: Stylophora sp. (Specific Strain)
  • Primary Common Name: Pink Flamingo Stylophora
  • Other Common Names: Hot Pink Stylophora, Pink Cat's Paw Coral
  • Origin: Cultured/Maricultured (Originals from the Indo-Pacific)
  • Max Size: Forms large, dense, bushy colonies (8+ inches tall/wide)
  • Temperament: Peaceful (Fast-growing and requires physical space)
  • Diet: Photosynthetic and Carnivore (Benefits from target feeding)
  • Minimum Tank Size: 40 Gallons (Established SPS or Mixed Reef)
  • Reef Compatibility: Yes (Excellent, hardy branching SPS option)
  • Care Level: Easy to Moderate

products.title: Rainbow Stylocoeniella
products.body_html:
Rainbow Stylocoeniella: The Polychromatic Deepwater Encruster

The Rainbow Stylocoeniella (Stylocoeniella species) is a unique and incredibly durable collector strain of Small Polyp Stony (SPS) coral. Stylocoeniella, often called "Cat's Paw Coral," is highly regarded for its resilience, small polyp size, and its ability to add vibrant color to shaded or lower-light regions of the reef tank.

This specific strain is named "Rainbow" because of its complex, polychromatic fluorescence. It typically features a base tissue of deep purple, dark blue, or dark brown that encrusts the rockwork. This dark base is densely dotted with numerous, tiny polyps that display a brilliant mix of colors, often including neon green, bright yellow, and hot pink/magenta. This combination of a dark body and multi-colored fluorescent polyps creates a stunning, speckled, "rainbow" effect.

The Rainbow Stylocoeniella grows in a thickly encrusting or forming small, dense, knobby bumps. It is considered an Easy care SPS coral. It is extremely robust, highly forgiving of moderate nutrient levels, and is a perfect choice for reefers looking for a hardy coral with exceptional multi-color fluorescence.

Diet & Compatibility:

  • Diet: This coral is primarily Photosynthetic (deriving most energy from symbiotic algae, zooxanthellae) but is also an opportunistic Carnivore.
  • Feeding: Supplemental feeding is highly beneficial for supporting robust polyp extension and accelerating growth. Its micro-polyps readily capture fine particulate foods. Target feed 2-3 times per week with fine foods, such as cyclops, rotifers, or commercial coral powders and amino acid solutions.
  • Reef Compatibility: It is Reef Safe and generally Passive. Stylocoeniella lacks potent sweeper tentacles and is non-aggressive. It is highly durable and an excellent choice for encrusting over base rock, requiring minimal space from neighboring corals.
  • Temperament: Peaceful toward fish and motile invertebrates, and Passive toward other corals. It should be positioned on lower or shaded rockwork where it receives Low to Moderate PAR lighting (50-250 PAR), where its fluorescence pops the most, and moderate, non-turbulent water flow to ensure clean surfaces.
  • Water Requirements:** Requires stable parameters. The tank should be mature with low to moderate nutrient levels (Nitrate < 10 ppm, Phosphate < 0.1 ppm). Critical parameters: Alkalinity (dKH 8.0-9.5), Calcium (400-450 ppm), and Magnesium (1300-1450 ppm).

Quick Facts

  • Scientific Name: Stylocoeniella sp. (Specific Strain)
  • Primary Common Name: Rainbow Stylocoeniella
  • Other Common Names: Multi-Color Stylocoeniella, Polychromatic Jasmine Coral
  • Origin: Cultured/Maricultured (Originals from the Indo-Pacific)
  • Max Size: Forms thick encrusting mats or small stubby colonies
  • Temperament: Peaceful (Passive, very hardy SPS option)
  • Diet: Photosynthetic and Carnivore (Benefits from target feeding)
  • Minimum Tank Size: 20 Gallons (Established reef)
  • Reef Compatibility: Yes (Excellent for lower-light zones)
  • Care Level: Easy

products.title: Candy Apple Stylocoeniella
products.body_html:
Candy Apple Stylocoeniella: The Intense Crimson Encruster

The Candy Apple Stylocoeniella (Stylocoeniella species) is a highly desirable and exceptionally durable collector strain of Small Polyp Stony (SPS) coral. Stylocoeniella, often called "Cat's Paw Coral" or "Jasmine Coral," is recognized for its tiny, densely packed polyps and its remarkable ability to thrive in a wide range of lighting conditions, particularly lower PAR.

This specific strain is named "Candy Apple" for its vivid, intense, and glossy red coloration. It features a base tissue of deep, saturated crimson or bright candy-apple red that encrusts the rockwork. The polyps are typically the same color or a slightly contrasting neon pink or rose red, creating a consistent and brilliant red showpiece that truly glows under appropriate lighting.

The Candy Apple Stylocoeniella grows in a thickly encrusting or forming short, stubby, finger-like branches. It is considered an Easy care SPS coral. It is highly robust, forgiving of moderate nutrient levels, and is a persistent, if initially slow, colonizer, making it a perfect coral for beginners stepping into SPS territory.

Diet & Compatibility:

  • Diet: This coral is primarily Photosynthetic (deriving most energy from symbiotic algae, zooxanthellae) but is also an opportunistic Carnivore.

Target feed 2-3 times per week with fine foods, such as cyclops, rotifers, or commercial coral powders and amino acid solutions.
  • Reef Compatibility: It is Reef Safe and generally Passive. Stylocoeniella lacks potent sweeper tentacles and is non-aggressive. It is highly durable and an excellent choice for encrusting over base rock, but due to its slow initial growth, it should be protected from larger, more aggressive corals that may sting or overgrow it.

It should be positioned on lower or mid-level rockwork where it receives Low to Moderate PAR lighting (50-250 PAR), which helps maintain the deep red color, and moderate, non-turbulent water flow to ensure clean surfaces.

products.title: Burning Banana Stylocoeniella
products.body_html:
Burning Banana Stylocoeniella: The Fluorescent Yellow-Green Encruster

The Burning Banana Stylocoeniella (Stylocoeniella species) is a highly fluorescent and exceptionally hardy collector strain of Small Polyp Stony (SPS) coral. Stylocoeniella, often called "Cat's Paw Coral" or "Jasmine Coral," is known for its tiny, densely packed polyps and its remarkable ability to thrive in shaded or lower-light areas of the reef tank.

This dark base is spectacularly contrasted by numerous, fully extended polyps and growth tips in an intense neon yellow or banana-yellow. The Burning Banana Stylocoeniella grows in a thickly encrusting or forming short, stubby, finger-like branches. It should be positioned on lower or shaded rockwork where it receives Low to Moderate PAR lighting (50-250 PAR), which helps maintain the dark base color, and moderate, non-turbulent water flow to ensure clean surfaces.

products.title: Screaming Green Seriatopora
products.body_html:
Screaming Green Seriatopora: The Fluorescent Green Thicket

The Screaming Green Seriatopora (Seriatopora hystrix or similar species) is a highly fluorescent and resilient collector strain of Small Polyp Stony (SPS) coral. Seriatopora, commonly known as "Bird's Nest Corals," are favored for their fine, dense, and sharp branching structure, and are excellent choices for adding quick growth to an SPS reef.

This specific strain is named for its unbelievably intense, nearly uniform neon green or fluorescent lime-green base tissue. The entire colony glows with saturation, often displaying a brilliant, almost day-glo appearance under actinic (blue) lighting. The polyps usually match the base color or are a slightly contrasting bright yellow, maintaining the coral's signature "screaming" green hue.

The Screaming Green Seriatopora grows in a dense, highly branched, and bushy morphology, forming intricate thickets. It is considered a Moderate care SPS coral. It is generally one of the hardier Seriatopora strains but still requires excellent water quality, high light, and strong, chaotic water flow to maintain its health, density, and vibrant color saturation.

Diet & Compatibility:

  • Diet: This coral is primarily Photosynthetic (deriving most energy from symbiotic algae, zooxanthellae) but is also an opportunistic Carnivore.
  • Feeding: Supplemental feeding is highly beneficial for accelerating growth and enhancing color. Its small polyps readily capture fine particulate foods. Target feed 2-3 times per week with very fine foods, such as cyclops, rotifers, or commercial coral powders and amino acid solutions.
  • Reef Compatibility: It is Reef Safe and generally Passive. It lacks potent sweeper tentacles, but due to its fast and dense growth, it can easily shade out or physically irritate neighboring corals. Ensure it has ample space (3-4 inches) and vertical clearance.
  • Temperament: Peaceful toward fish and motile invertebrates, and Passive toward other corals. It should be positioned on high rockwork where it receives High PAR lighting (300-450+ PAR) and strong, chaotic, turbulent water flow. High flow is essential to prevent detritus buildup within the dense branches, which can lead to tissue loss.
  • Water Requirements: Requires stable parameters. The tank should be mature with very low nutrient levels (Nitrate < 5 ppm, Phosphate < 0.1 ppm). Consistency in alkalinity is paramount. Critical parameters: Alkalinity (dKH 8.0-9.5), Calcium (400-450 ppm), and Magnesium (1300-1450 ppm).

Quick Facts

  • Scientific Name: Seriatopora hystrix (Specific Strain)
  • Primary Common Name: Screaming Green Seriatopora
  • Other Common Names: Neon Green Bird's Nest, Electric Green Seriatopora
  • Origin: Cultured/Maricultured (Originals from the Indo-Pacific)
  • Max Size: Forms large, dense, bushy colonies (8+ inches tall/wide)
  • Temperament: Peaceful (Fast-growing and requires physical space)
  • Diet: Photosynthetic and Carnivore (Benefits from target feeding)
  • Minimum Tank Size: 40 Gallons (Established SPS or Mixed Reef)
  • Reef Compatibility: Yes (Excellent, fast-growing SPS option)
  • Care Level: Moderate

products.title: Grinch Porites
products.body_html:
Grinch Porites: The Neon Green and Red Christmas Coral

The Grinch Porites (Porites species) is a highly desirable, colorful, and resilient collector strain of Small Polyp Stony (SPS) coral. Porites species, often called "Finger Corals" or "Jewel Corals," are highly regarded for their micro-polyp structure and exceptional hardiness in captive environments.

This specific strain is named "Grinch" due to its classic high-contrast holiday coloration. It typically features a base tissue of bright, vibrant neon green or lime-green. This base is speckled with numerous, contrasting, minute polyps in hot pink, magenta, or bright red. The overall effect is a highly fluorescent green canvas dotted with intensely colored polyps, making it a spectacular showpiece.

The Grinch Porites commonly grows in an encrusting or columnar/finger-like morphology. It is considered an Easy to Moderate care SPS coral and is one of the most durable SPS options, thriving even in systems that are still maturing. It requires consistent water chemistry, moderate to high light, and ample water flow to maximize its color and steady, durable growth.

Diet & Compatibility:

  • Diet: This coral is primarily Photosynthetic (deriving most energy from symbiotic algae, zooxanthellae) but is also an opportunistic Carnivore.
  • Feeding: Supplemental feeding is recommended and highly beneficial, especially for promoting faster tissue growth. Its micro-polyps readily capture very fine particulate foods. Target feed 2-3 times per week with fine foods, such as cyclops, rotifers, or commercial coral powders and amino acid solutions.
  • Reef Compatibility: It is Reef Safe and generally Passive. Porites lacks long sweeper tentacles and is non-aggressive. While it is tough, its slow initial growth means it should be placed away from aggressive corals (like LPS) that could sting or grow over it.
  • Temperament: Peaceful toward fish and motile invertebrates, and Passive toward other corals. It should be positioned on mid-to-high rockwork where it receives Moderate to High PAR lighting (200-400 PAR) and moderate to strong, turbulent water flow to ensure polyp extension and maintain clean surfaces.
  • Water Requirements: Requires stable parameters. The tank should be mature with very low nutrient levels (Nitrate < 5 ppm, Phosphate < 0.1 ppm). Critical parameters: Alkalinity (dKH 8.0-9.5), Calcium (400-450 ppm), and Magnesium (1300-1450 ppm).

Quick Facts

  • Scientific Name: Porites sp. (Specific Strain)
  • Primary Common Name: Grinch Porites
  • Other Common Names: Green Base Red Polyp Porites, Christmas Porites
  • Origin: Cultured/Maricultured (Originals from the Indo-Pacific)
  • Max Size: Can form massive colonies or large branching structures (12+ inches)
  • Temperament: Peaceful (Passive, very durable coral)
  • Diet: Photosynthetic and Carnivore (Benefits from fine particulate feeding)
  • Minimum Tank Size: 40 Gallons (Established SPS or Mixed Reef)
  • Reef Compatibility: Yes (Excellent, hardy SPS option)
  • Care Level: Easy to Moderate

products.title: Darth Maul Porites
products.body_html:
Darth Maul Porites: The Deep Red and Neon Yellow Encruster

The Darth Maul Porites (Porites species) is a highly desirable and exceptionally hardy collector strain of Small Polyp Stony (SPS) coral. Porites species, often called "Finger Corals" or "Jewel Corals," are foundational corals known for their durability and dense, micro-polyp structure.

This specific strain is named after the iconic Star Wars character for its dramatic, high-contrast coloration. It typically features a base tissue of deep, rich red, maroon, or dark purple. This base is speckled with numerous, contrasting, minute polyps in bright neon yellow or chartreuse green. The combination creates an intense, speckled pattern that fluoresces brilliantly under blue-spectrum lighting.

The Darth Maul Porites commonly grows in an encrusting or columnar/finger-like morphology, depending on the specific species. It is considered an Easy to Moderate care SPS coral and is one of the toughest corals available for a new or established reef. It is slow to start but requires consistent water chemistry, moderate to high light, and ample water flow to thrive.

Diet & Compatibility:

  • Diet: This coral is primarily Photosynthetic (deriving most energy from symbiotic algae, zooxanthellae) but is also an opportunistic Carnivore.
  • Feeding: Supplemental feeding is recommended and highly beneficial, especially for newly introduced frags. Its micro-polyps readily capture very fine particulate foods. Target feed 2-3 times per week with fine foods, such as cyclops, rotifers, or commercial coral powders and amino acid solutions.
  • Reef Compatibility: It is Reef Safe and generally Passive. Porites lacks long sweeper tentacles and is non-aggressive. It is highly durable and resistant to many pests, but its slow initial growth means it should be protected from being stung or overgrown by aggressive LPS or fast-plating Montipora.
  • Temperament: Peaceful toward fish and motile invertebrates, and Passive toward other corals. It should be positioned on mid-to-high rockwork where it receives Moderate to High PAR lighting (200-400 PAR) and moderate to strong, turbulent water flow to ensure proper nutrient exchange and polyp extension.
  • Water Requirements: Requires stable parameters. The tank should be mature with very low nutrient levels (Nitrate < 5 ppm, Phosphate < 0.1 ppm). Critical parameters: Alkalinity (dKH 8.0-9.5), Calcium (400-450 ppm), and Magnesium (1300-1450 ppm).

Quick Facts

  • Scientific Name: Porites sp. (Specific Strain)
  • Primary Common Name: Darth Maul Porites
  • Other Common Names: Red Base Yellow Polyp Porites, Sith Lord Porites
  • Origin: Cultured/Maricultured (Originals from the Indo-Pacific)
  • Max Size: Can form massive colonies or large branching fingers (12+ inches)
  • Temperament: Peaceful (Passive, very slow initial growth)
  • Diet: Photosynthetic and Carnivore (Benefits from fine particulate feeding)
  • Minimum Tank Size: 40 Gallons (Established SPS or Mixed Reef)
  • Reef Compatibility: Yes (Excellent, durable SPS option)
  • Care Level: Easy to Moderate

products.title: Pinky and the Brain Pocillopora
products.body_html:
Pinky and the Brain Pocillopora: The Highly Contrasting Pink and Green Coral

The Pinky and the Brain Pocillopora (Pocillopora species) is a unique and sought-after collector strain of Small Polyp Stony (SPS) coral. Pocillopora, commonly known as "Cauliflower Corals" or "Bush Corals," are valued for their durability, rapid growth, and knobby, thicket-forming structure.

This specific strain is named after the iconic cartoon duo for its high-contrast color scheme. It typically exhibits a base tissue that ranges from bright bubblegum pink, hot pink, or magenta. This vibrant pink base is sharply contrasted by prominent, extended polyps in brilliant neon green or chartreuse. The dual coloration is highly fluorescent and creates a striking "pink brain, green polyps" effect.

The Pinky and the Brain Pocillopora grows in a dense, branching, and columnar morphology, forming thick, knobby bushes. It is considered an Easy to Moderate care SPS coral, making it a robust addition to a mixed or SPS-dominant tank. It requires consistent water chemistry, high light, and strong, chaotic water flow to maintain its dense structure and maximum color saturation.

Diet & Compatibility:

  • Diet: This coral is primarily Photosynthetic (deriving most energy from symbiotic algae, zooxanthellae) but is also an opportunistic Carnivore.
  • Feeding: Supplemental feeding is recommended and highly beneficial for accelerating growth and ensuring the deepest color saturation. Its small, sticky polyps readily capture fine particulate foods. Target feed 2-3 times per week with very fine foods, such as cyclops, rotifers, or commercial coral powders and amino acid solutions.
  • Reef Compatibility: It is Reef Safe but generally Semi-Aggressive. While it lacks long sweeper tentacles, Pocillopora can produce long, clear mesenterial filaments to sting and digest neighbors if they touch or are too close. Due to its fast growth and potential mild aggression, ensure it has sufficient space (at least 2 inches) from sensitive corals.
  • Temperament: Peaceful toward fish and motile invertebrates, and Semi-Aggressive/Competitive toward other corals. It should be positioned on high rockwork where it receives High PAR lighting (300-450 PAR) and strong, non-laminar, turbulent water flow to support its high metabolic rate and prevent stagnant water within the colony's dense structure.
  • Water Requirements: Requires stable parameters. The tank should be mature with very low nutrient levels (Nitrate < 5 ppm, Phosphate < 0.1 ppm). Critical parameters: Alkalinity (dKH 8.0-9.5), Calcium (400-450 ppm), and Magnesium (1300-1450 ppm).

Quick Facts

  • Scientific Name: Pocillopora sp. (Specific Strain)
  • Primary Common Name: Pinky and the Brain Pocillopora
  • Other Common Names: Pink Base Green Polyp Pocillopora, Pink Bush Coral
  • Origin: Cultured/Maricultured (Originals from the Indo-Pacific)
  • Max Size: Can form large, dense bushes (8+ inches tall)
  • Temperament: Semi-Aggressive (Fast-growing and uses mesenterial filaments)
  • Diet: Photosynthetic and Carnivore (Benefits from target feeding)
  • Minimum Tank Size: 40 Gallons (Established SPS or Mixed Reef)
  • Reef Compatibility: Yes (Excellent, hardy branching SPS option)
  • Care Level: Easy to Moderate

products.title: Pickle Juice Pocillopora
products.body_html:
Pickle Juice Pocillopora: The Neon Yellow-Green Thicket

The Pickle Juice Pocillopora (Pocillopora species) is a vibrant and highly fluorescent collector strain of Small Polyp Stony (SPS) coral. Pocillopora, commonly known as "Cauliflower Corals" or "Bush Corals," are highly prized for their hardiness, rapid growth, and distinct knobby, thicket-forming structure.

This specific strain is named "Pickle Juice" for its uniform, intense neon yellow or lime-green base tissue that covers the entire colony. The polyps and growth tips are often the same highly saturated neon green, giving the coral a consistent, brilliant, and glowing appearance, especially when viewed under high-actinic (blue) lighting.

The Pickle Juice Pocillopora grows in a dense, branching, and columnar morphology, forming thick, knobby bushes. It is considered an Easy to Moderate care SPS coral and is often recommended as a great introductory SPS. It requires consistent water chemistry, high light, and strong, chaotic water flow to maintain its dense structure and brilliant coloration.

Diet & Compatibility:

  • Diet: This coral is primarily Photosynthetic (deriving most energy from symbiotic algae, zooxanthellae) but is also an opportunistic Carnivore.
  • Feeding: Supplemental feeding is recommended and highly beneficial for accelerating growth and ensuring the deepest color saturation. Its small, sticky polyps readily capture fine particulate foods. Target feed 2-3 times per week with very fine foods, such as cyclops, rotifers, or commercial coral powders and amino acid solutions.
  • Reef Compatibility: It is Reef Safe but generally Semi-Aggressive. While it lacks long sweeper tentacles, Pocillopora can produce long, clear mesenterial filaments to sting and digest neighbors if they touch or are too close. Due to its fast growth and potential mild aggression, ensure it has sufficient space (at least 2 inches) from sensitive corals.
  • Temperament: Peaceful toward fish and motile invertebrates, and Semi-Aggressive/Competitive toward other corals. It should be positioned on high rockwork where it receives High PAR lighting (300-450 PAR) and strong, non-laminar, turbulent water flow to support its high metabolic rate and prevent stagnant water within the colony's dense structure.
  • Water Requirements: Requires stable parameters. The tank should be mature with very low nutrient levels (Nitrate < 5 ppm, Phosphate < 0.1 ppm). Critical parameters: Alkalinity (dKH 8.0-9.5), Calcium (400-450 ppm), and Magnesium (1300-1450 ppm).

Quick Facts

  • Scientific Name: Pocillopora sp. (Specific Strain)
  • Primary Common Name: Pickle Juice Pocillopora
  • Other Common Names: Neon Green Pocillopora, Lime Bush Coral
  • Origin: Cultured/Maricultured (Originals from the Indo-Pacific)
  • Max Size: Can form large, dense bushes (8+ inches tall)
  • Temperament: Semi-Aggressive (Fast-growing and uses mesenterial filaments)
  • Diet: Photosynthetic and Carnivore (Benefits from target feeding)
  • Minimum Tank Size: 40 Gallons (Established SPS or Mixed Reef)
  • Reef Compatibility: Yes (Excellent, hardy branching SPS option)
  • Care Level: Easy to Moderate

products.title: Frozone Pocillopora
products.body_html:
Frozone Pocillopora: The Icy Blue-Tip Coral

The Frozone Pocillopora (Pocillopora damicornis or similar species) is a highly attractive and distinctive collector strain of Small Polyp Stony (SPS) coral. Pocillopora, commonly known as "Cauliflower Corals" or "Bush Corals," are highly prized for their hardiness, rapid growth, and knobby, thicket-forming structure.

This specific strain is named "Frozone" for its cool, icy coloration. It typically features a base tissue that ranges from pale pink or light lavender which is dramatically contrasted by intense, bright electric blue or icy blue growth tips (axials) and polyps. The blue fluorescence of the tips makes the coral glow brilliantly under actinic (blue) lighting, resembling frozen ice.

The Frozone Pocillopora grows in a dense, branching, and columnar morphology, forming thick, knobby bushes. It is considered an Easy to Moderate care SPS coral. It is one of the most forgiving SPS corals, often used to test new SPS systems. It requires consistent water chemistry, high light, and strong, chaotic water flow to maintain its dense structure and brilliant tip coloration.

Diet & Compatibility:

  • Diet: This coral is primarily Photosynthetic (deriving most energy from symbiotic algae, zooxanthellae) but is also an opportunistic Carnivore.
  • Feeding: Supplemental feeding is recommended and highly beneficial for accelerating growth and ensuring the deepest color saturation. Its small, sticky polyps readily capture fine particulate foods. Target feed 2-3 times per week with very fine foods, such as cyclops, rotifers, or commercial coral powders and amino acid solutions.
  • Reef Compatibility: It is Reef Safe but generally Semi-Aggressive. While it lacks long sweeper tentacles, Pocillopora can produce long, clear mesenterial filaments to sting and digest neighbors if they touch or are too close. Due to its fast growth and potential mild aggression, ensure it has sufficient space (at least 2 inches) from sensitive corals.
  • Temperament: Peaceful toward fish and motile invertebrates, and Semi-Aggressive/Competitive toward other corals. It should be positioned on high rockwork where it receives High PAR lighting (300-450 PAR) and strong, non-laminar, turbulent water flow to support its high metabolic rate and prevent stagnant water within the colony's dense structure.
  • Water Requirements: Requires stable parameters. The tank should be mature with very low nutrient levels (Nitrate < 5 ppm, Phosphate < 0.1 ppm). Critical parameters: Alkalinity (dKH 8.0-9.5), Calcium (400-450 ppm), and Magnesium (1300-1450 ppm).

Quick Facts

  • Scientific Name: Pocillopora sp. (Specific Strain)
  • Primary Common Name: Frozone Pocillopora
  • Other Common Names: Icy Blue Pocillopora, Blue Tip Bush Coral
  • Origin: Cultured/Maricultured (Originals from the Indo-Pacific)
  • Max Size: Can form large, dense bushes (8+ inches tall)
  • Temperament: Semi-Aggressive (Fast-growing and uses mesenterial filaments)
  • Diet: Photosynthetic and Carnivore (Benefits from target feeding)
  • Minimum Tank Size: 40 Gallons (Established SPS or Mixed Reef)
  • Reef Compatibility: Yes (Excellent, hardy branching SPS option)
  • Care Level: Easy to Moderate

products.title: Mint Chip Pavona
products.body_html:
Mint Chip Pavona: The Green and Brown Cactus Coral

The Mint Chip Pavona (Pavona species) is a distinctive and durable collector strain of Small Polyp Stony (SPS) coral. Pavona species, often referred to as "Cactus Corals" or "Lettuce Corals," are highly prized for their unique texture, resilience, and striking colors when properly cared for.

This specific strain is named "Mint Chip" for its resemblance to the ice cream flavor. It typically features a base tissue that ranges from light brown, tan, or deep forest green. This base color is heavily contrasted by numerous, often sparse, polyps in a vibrant neon green or mint green. The small, bright green polyps scattered across the darker base tissue create the signature "chip" effect.

The Mint Chip Pavona commonly grows in an encrusting, plating, or scrolling morphology. It is considered an Easy to Moderate care SPS coral, often cited as one of the hardiest SPS options available. It requires consistent water chemistry, moderate to high light, and ample water flow to maintain its rapid growth and brilliant polyp extension.

Diet & Compatibility:

  • Diet: This coral is primarily Photosynthetic (deriving most energy from symbiotic algae, zooxanthellae) but is also an opportunistic Carnivore.
  • Feeding: Supplemental feeding is recommended and highly beneficial for accelerating growth and enhancing color. Its small polyps readily capture fine particulate foods. Target feed 2-3 times per week with very fine foods, such as cyclops, rotifers, or commercial coral powders and amino acid solutions.
  • Reef Compatibility: It is Reef Safe and generally Semi-Aggressive. While Pavona does not extend long sweeper tentacles, it is an aggressive encruster. It will rapidly grow over rockwork and can sting and grow over adjacent, slower-moving corals. Proper spacing is essential to prevent territorial disputes.
  • Temperament: Peaceful toward fish and motile invertebrates, and Semi-Aggressive/Competitive toward other corals. It should be positioned on mid-level rockwork where it receives Moderate to High PAR lighting (150-350 PAR) and strong, non-laminar, turbulent water flow to prevent detritus buildup in its folds and ensure proper nutrient exchange.
  • Water Requirements: Requires stable parameters. The tank should be mature with low to moderate nutrient levels (Nitrate < 10 ppm, Phosphate < 0.1 ppm). Critical parameters: Alkalinity (dKH 8.0-9.5), Calcium (400-450 ppm), and Magnesium (1300-1450 ppm).

Quick Facts

  • Scientific Name: Pavona sp. (Specific Strain)
  • Primary Common Name: Mint Chip Pavona
  • Other Common Names: Mint Chip Cactus Coral, Green Polyp Pavona
  • Origin: Cultured/Maricultured (Originals from the Indo-Pacific)
  • Max Size: Can form large encrusting/scrolling colonies (12+ inches in diameter)
  • Temperament: Semi-Aggressive (Highly encrusting and competitive)
  • Diet: Photosynthetic and Carnivore (Benefits from target feeding)
  • Minimum Tank Size: 40 Gallons (Established SPS or Mixed Reef)
  • Reef Compatibility: Yes (Excellent, hardy SPS option, but give it space)
  • Care Level: Easy to Moderate

products.title: Tropic Thunder Montipora
products.body_html:
Tropic Thunder Montipora: The Neon Multi-Color Plater

The Tropic Thunder Montipora (Montipora species) is a highly attractive and distinctive collector strain of Small Polyp Stony (SPS) coral. Montipora species are highly regarded for their rapid growth and stability in captive reef systems.

This specific strain is named "Tropic Thunder" for its bright, intense, and complex polychromatic coloration, suggesting a chaotic mix of vibrant tropical hues. It typically exhibits a base tissue that ranges from deep purple, blue, or mauve. This base is dramatically contrasted by numerous, often sparse, polyps in intense neon green or bright yellow. Crucially, the growth edge often displays a vivid electric pink or reddish band, completing the tri-color profile.

The Tropic Thunder Montipora commonly grows in an encrusting or thinly plating morphology. It is considered a Moderate care SPS coral. It is generally hardier than Acropora but requires consistent water chemistry, high light, and ample water flow to maintain its rapid growth and brilliant color saturation.

Diet & Compatibility:

  • Diet: This coral is primarily Photosynthetic (deriving most energy from symbiotic algae, zooxanthellae) but is also an opportunistic Carnivore.
  • Feeding: Supplemental feeding is highly beneficial for faster growth and robust health. Its small polyps readily capture fine particulate foods. Target feed 2-3 times per week with very fine foods, such as cyclops, rotifers, or commercial coral powders and amino acid solutions.
  • Reef Compatibility: It is Reef Safe and generally Passive. Montipora lacks powerful sweeper tentacles. However, as a fast-encrusting/plating coral, it can quickly grow over and aggressively shade out lower-lying or slower-growing corals. Proper spacing is essential to prevent territorial disputes via shading.
  • Temperament: Peaceful toward fish and motile invertebrates, and Passive toward other corals. It should be positioned on mid-to-high rockwork where it receives High PAR lighting (250-450 PAR) and strong, non-laminar, turbulent water flow to prevent detritus buildup on its surfaces.
  • Water Requirements: Requires stable parameters. The tank should be mature with very low nutrient levels (Nitrate < 5 ppm, Phosphate < 0.1 ppm). Critical parameters: Alkalinity (dKH 8.0-9.5), Calcium (400-450 ppm), and Magnesium (1300-1450 ppm).

Quick Facts

  • Scientific Name: Montipora sp. (Specific Strain)
  • Primary Common Name: Tropic Thunder Montipora
  • Other Common Names: Tri-Color Plating Monti, Blue/Green/Pink Monti
  • Origin: Cultured/Maricultured (Originals from the Indo-Pacific)
  • Max Size: Can form large encrusting colonies/plates (12+ inches in diameter)
  • Temperament: Peaceful (Passive, but fast-growing and competitive via shading)
  • Diet: Photosynthetic and Carnivore (Benefits from target feeding)
  • Minimum Tank Size: 40 Gallons (Established SPS or Mixed Reef)
  • Reef Compatibility: Yes (Excellent, non-aggressive SPS option)
  • Care Level: Moderate

products.title: Bacopa
products.body_html:
Bacopa

Bacopa are long narrow stemmed plants with small round green leaves. Bacopa will flower in the aquarium and are quite pretty. Each order comes with 5 plants and a lead weight for easy placement. These plants are somewhat hardy, but need good light and water conditions to grow and live longer than a couple months.

  • Scientific Name: Bacopa Caroliniana
  • Origin: Florida farm
  • Shipping Size: Approx. 8 inches

products.title: Achara Catfish
products.body_html:
Achara Catfish

Not all catfish look pretty when they grow, but the Achara Catfish will keep it's leopard pattern throughout its life. Achara Catfish, or Sailfin Pim, are caught in the Amazon basin on the Peru side, usually about three to four inches. These are peaceful catfish that will grow to 24 inches, and like most catfish they will swallow most edibles that fit in their mouth, including slow moving small fish. These fish have been bred with Tiger Shovelnose to create a tank hardy catfish that looks more like a classic Tiger shovelnose, but the Achara referenced here are the real McCoy.

  • Scientific name: Leiarius marmoratus
  • Origin: Peru
  • Lifespan: 20 years
  • Max size: 24 inches
  • Food: Frozen, live feeders, pellets
  • Shipping Size: Approx. 3 inch

products.title: Java Fern on Lava Rock
products.body_html:
Java Fern on Lava Rock

The rusty red color of lava rock goes together with the deep green color of the hardy Java Fern. Porous surface of lava rock offers a haven for roots and allows beneficial bacteria to grow within the pores.

Quick Facts

  • Scientific Name:  Leptochilus pteropus
  • Max Height (Submerged): 10 inches
  • Care Level: Beginner / Very Easy
  • Lighting: Low to Moderate
  • CO₂ Injection: Not Required (Benefits from CO2, but thrives without it)
  • Growth Rate: Very Slow
  • Temperature Range: 68−86°F ( 20−30°C )
  • Water pH: 5.5 - 8.0 (Highly Adaptable)
  • Placement: Anywhere

products.title: Java Fern on Driftwood
products.body_html:
Java Fern on Driftwood

Thin deep green to black leaves of the Java Fern accent the brown wood colors and the plant hold to the wood tightly. Low to mid level lighting is fine for these hardy plants.

Quick Facts

  • Scientific Name:  Leptochilus pteropus
  • Max Height (Submerged): 10 inches
  • Care Level: Beginner / Very Easy
  • Lighting: Low to Moderate
  • CO₂ Injection: Not Required (Benefits from CO2, but thrives without it)

  • Temperature Range: 68−86°F ( 20−30°C )
  • Water pH: 5.5 - 8.0 (Highly Adaptable)
  • Placement: Anywhere

products.title: Mantis Shrimp
products.body_html:
Spot-tail Mantis Shrimp: The Spearer Predator

The Spot-tail Mantis Shrimp (Squilla mantis), also known by its regional names Canocchia or Pacchero, is a large, highly specialized crustacean belonging to the order Stomatopoda. Unlike the brightly colored 'smasher' mantis shrimp often seen in the hobby, S. mantis is a 'spearer' type, characterized by a flattened, segmented body and long, spiny, raptorial appendages designed to impale soft-bodied prey.

Native to the shallow coastal areas of the Mediterranean Sea and the Eastern Atlantic, this species is typically a drabber color, often pale green or translucent with a distinctive dark spot (ocellus) on its tail or telson. It is an ambush predator that spends the vast majority of its time hidden within elaborate, U-shaped burrows excavated in deep mud or fine sand.

The Spot-tail Mantis Shrimp is an opportunistic, territorial, and extremely effective hunter. It should never be kept in a reef or mixed-species tank, as it will systematically prey on and kill fish, shrimp, crabs, and other invertebrates. It must be housed in a species-specific aquarium with secure, non-glass walls (or very thick glass) to prevent potential damage from its powerful strike.

Diet & Compatibility:

  • Diet: This mantis shrimp is a dedicated Carnivore / Opportunistic Predator. In the wild, it feeds on smaller crustaceans, fish, worms (polychaetes), and mollusks (bivalves and squid).
  • Feeding: In captivity, feed it 3-4 times per week with meaty foods, such as frozen shrimp, thawed clams, squid, or pieces of fish. They are intelligent and can be target-fed using tongs, which is safer for the keeper. Providing food with a hard outer shell (like whole frozen shrimp) allows them to exercise their hunting skills.
  • Reef Compatibility: No. Mantis shrimps are completely incompatible with nearly all other aquarium life, including corals, fish, and invertebrates. They are notorious for killing tank mates and can destabilize rockwork.
  • Temperament: Extremely Aggressive and Predatory. They are highly territorial and must be kept singly. Their "spearing" strike is powerful and fast enough to severely injure human fingers (earning them the nickname "thumb splitters"). Extreme caution and protective gloves are mandatory when performing tank maintenance.
  • Tank Requirements: Requires a minimum 30-gallon species-only tank with a deep substrate (at least 6-8 inches) of fine sand mixed with rubble to allow for proper burrow construction. PVC pipes can be buried in the substrate to provide a pre-made, stable home. The tank should have thick acrylic or tempered glass walls and a tight-fitting lid (as they are known jumpers).

Quick Facts

  • Scientific Name: Squilla mantis
  • Primary Common Name: Spot-tail Mantis Shrimp
  • Other Common Names: Canocchia, Pacchero, Sea Locust, Thumb Splitter
  • Origin: Mediterranean Sea, Eastern Atlantic Ocean
  • Max Size: 7-10 inches (18-25 cm)
  • Temperament: Extremely Aggressive / Predatory (Spearer)
  • Diet: Carnivore (Crustaceans, fish, worms)
  • Minimum Tank Size: 30 Gallons (Species-Only)
  • Reef Compatibility: No (Will destroy tank life)
  • Danger: Highly Dangerous (Spearing appendage can cause deep lacerations)

products.title: Yellow Parrot Cichlid
products.body_html:
Yellow Parrot Cichlid

The most prevalent theory as to how this color came about for the Yellow Parrot Cichlid from Taiwan is through selective breeding of Midas and Severum Cichlids, but there is probably more to the story. Regardless, these bright yellow hybrids offer a beautiful alternative to an already big menu of exotic hybrid parrot cichlids. Like most parrots, their behavior is unpredictable and can run from peaceful to aggressive. Hybrids are a mixed bag in more ways than one.

  • Scientific Name: Psumbuidi madiupi
  • Origin:  Taiwan - hybrid
  • Lifespan: 8 years
  • Max Size: 10 inches
  • Food: Flake, live, frozen, pellet
  • Shipping Size:  Approx. 3 inches

products.title: Rummynose Tetra (School of 6)
products.body_html:
Rummynose Tetra (School of 6)

The rummynose tetra is a tight schooling fish which makes for some dramatic motions as all six of these fish move about in unison.

products.title: Blue Leopard Angelfish (School of 3)
products.body_html:
Blue Leopard Angelfish (School of 3)

Technically not schooling fish, two of these fish will eventually pair up. The interactions of a small group of angels makes an aquarium a heavenly place.

products.title: Black Convict Cichlid (School of 3)
products.body_html:
Black Convict Cichlid (School of 3)

When black convicts get to their adult stage they turn aggressive, so if a few of them grow up together at least they can have a couple adult friends in the tank,

products.title: Angel Veil Ram (School of 3)
products.body_html:
Angel Veil Ram (School of 3)

A small group of Veil Rams in a large tank is always a good idea. Individuals tend to stress so a couple colleagues, even if the butt heads from time to time, will allow these dwarf cichlids to settle in the aquarium.

products.title: Bolivian Ram (Group of 3)
products.body_html:
Bolivian Ram (Group of 3)

Three Bolivian Rams are a nice grouping for a small or mid-size fish tank where you can easily observe the social interactions.

products.title: Kribensis Cichlid (Group of 3)
products.body_html:
Kribensis Cichlid (Group of 3)

Though Kribensis are not schoolers, they do form a communal area in the aquarium - and the lake - so keeping them in small groups keeps them happy.

products.title: Glass Bloodfin Tetra (School of 6)
products.body_html:
Glass Bloodfin Tetra (School of 6)

Elegant clear bodies and bright red tails create a splendid school for any aquarium.

products.title: Purple Emperor Tetra (School of 6)
products.body_html:
Purple Emperor Tetra (School of 6)

Purple is such an unusual color in the wild might as well take advantage of this species and fill the tank with a complete school.

products.title: Red Blue Colombian Tetra (School of 6)
products.body_html:
Red Blue Colombian Tetra (School of 6)

The hardy Red and Blue Colombian Tetra will grow to 3 inches, so this group needs a midsize to large tank at maturity.

products.title: Gold Marble Angelfish (Group of 3)
products.body_html:
Gold Marble Angelfish (Group of 3)

These three small to medium angelfish will grow to a nice size together and eventually two of them will pair up - happens to all of us.

products.title: Albino Angelfish (School of 3)
products.body_html:
Albino Angelfish (School of 3)

A group of angelfish is called a school or herd, but why confuse the issue, we just stick with good old school. Each order comes with three medium albino angelfish, unsexed. So who knows what will happen when these juveniles grow up.

products.title: Rosy Barb (School of 6)
products.body_html:
Rosy Barb (School of 6)

Rosy Barbs are fast and tight schoolers and with the bright red males and the flashy copper females, this is a very reflective moving spectacle. A school of Rosy Barbs will need at least a ten gallon aquarium. More would be better.

products.title: Penta Barb (School of 5)
products.body_html: Penta Barb (School of 5)

products.title: Black Ruby Barb (School of 6)
products.body_html:
Black Ruby Barb (School of 6)

The purple and black hues of the ruby barb will almost shimmer as these active schoolers swim in a tight group about the tank.

products.title: Albino Tiger Barb (School of 6)
products.body_html:
Albino Tiger Barb (School of 6)

Each order comes with 6 barbs. A school of these very active fish will explore the entire tank space.

products.title: Pearl Danio (School of 6)
products.body_html:
Pearl Danio (School of 6)

A small group of these pearl danios will look like a quick moving translucent orb.

products.title: Chili Rasbora (School of 6)
products.body_html:
Chili Rasbora (School of 6)

Chili or Brigittae Rasbora are very small and really need to be kept in small schools. Six should be a good minimum number.

products.title: Brilliant Rasbora (School of 6)
products.body_html:
Brilliant Rasbora (School of 6)

The brilliant reflective long horizontal line on this rasbora is dramatic when a group are swimming and reflecting light together.

products.title: Clown Spot Dwarf Rasbora (School of 6)
products.body_html:
Clown Spot Dwarf Rasbora (School of 6)

Similar to the Chili Rasbora with a little less red, but more dominant spots, a group of these will bring out the patterns of these very nice nano Rasboras.

products.title: Neon Green Rasbora (School of 6)
products.body_html: Neon Green Rasbora (School of 6)

products.title: Scissortail Rasbora (School of 6)
products.body_html:
Scissortail Rasbora (School of 6)

Extremely hardy and adaptable to peaceful community fish tanks and semi aggressive cichlid tanks, a school of scissortails makes a nice attraction.

products.title: Blue Axelrodi Rasbora (School of 5)
products.body_html:
Blue Axelrodi Rasbora (School of 5)

The Deep Blue Rasboras, often called "Neon Blue Rasbora" make a beautiful school in a small aquarium. It's fortunate Dr. Axelrod discovered them.

products.title: Dwarf Coral Red Platy (School of 5)
products.body_html:
Dwarf Coral Red Platy (School of 5)

The Coral Red Platy is so bright, but small. Make a bigger bright red spectacle with five.

products.title: Red Wag Platy (School of 4)
products.body_html:
Red Wag Platy (School of 4)

Though a Platy can survive without undue stress alone, these are social fish and do better in small groups. Honestly - one Platy just swimming around by himself just sounds sad.

products.title: Albino Cory (Pack of 3)
products.body_html:
Albino Aeneus Cory (Pack of 3)

Technically any group of fish including Corydoras is called a school, but when you put a few of these little cory together the work "pack" seems more appropriate. Each order comes with three Albino Cory.

products.title: Panda Cory (Embarrassment of 3)
products.body_html: The Panda Cory has enough black on the dorsal fin and the body to give it its common name. Fortunately these cory do not require bamboo shoots, but scavenge uneaten food at the bottom of the aquarium. Panda cory do not grow very large and are tank raised in the aquatic trade. Wild panda cory from Venezuela are quite expensive but are rarely available. AquariumFish.com imports our tank raised panda cory from Singapore.

products.title: American Flagfish (School of 4)
products.body_html:
American Flagfish (School of 4)

The Flagfish will not normally school together, but appear to work in coordination as they explore their own little areas for food and hair algae.

products.title: 24 kt Lyre Tail Molly (Group of 4)
products.body_html:
Solid Gold 24kt Lyretail Molly (Group of 4)

A group of 4 mixed sex solid gold lyre tailed mollies will school, chase, and pair up in their new home, They just need someone to take them home.

products.title: Scarlet Gem Badis (School of 4)
products.body_html: Scarlet Gem Badis (School of 4)

products.title: Bumblebee Goby (Group of 3)
products.body_html:
Bumblebee Goby (Group of 3)

Bumblebee Gobies are notorious hiders, keep three so you are bound to catch site of them from time to time.

products.title: Vampire Crab (Consortium of 4)
products.body_html:
Vampire Crab (Consortium of 4)

If the tank is large at all with lots of hiding places, a group of four vampires might be best to ensure a couple can be spotted at any time. A group will be need to be feed a small amount once per day of blood worms or similar foods. But they can go for a bit without feeding, these crabs are great scavengers.

products.title: Otocinclus Algae Eater (Cleaning Crew of 4)
products.body_html:
Otocinclus Algae Eater (Cleaning Crew of 4)

These little guys will be all over the tank nibbling on whatever they can find.

products.title: Roseline Shark (School of 3)
products.body_html:
Roseline Shark (School of 3)

Three medium size Roseline Sharks will tend to swim in a tight group about the middle of the aquarium. These fish are peaceful and adapt well to medium and large aquariums.

products.title: Denisoni Barb (School of 3)
products.body_html:
Denisoni Barb (School of 3)

Three approximately 2 inch Barbs will quickly grow to nearly 6 inches, but remain compatible with medium to large species.

products.title: Ghost Glass Catfish (School of 4)
products.body_html:
Ghost Glass Catfish (School of 4)

A group of four Ghost Catfish will appear to hover together in various areas of the fish tank.

products.title: Apisto Cacatuoides (School of 4)
products.body_html:
Apisto Cacatuoides (School of 4)

Juvenile Cacatuoides (or Cockatoo) can be hard to sex. To ensure a nice mix of sexes and to keep these little dwarf cichlids feeling more at home a small group of four might be a good purchase for a larger community tank.

products.title: Dwarf Glass Fish (School of 5)
products.body_html:
Dwarf Glass Fish (School of 5)

Dwarf Glass Fish are shy schoolers and even if they are see through other fish can still see them. Allowing them to swim in a small group with greatly improve their disposition.

products.title: Blue Emperor Tetra (School of 6)
products.body_html:
Blue Emperor Tetra (School of 6)

Unless the Emperor Tetra is an adult male in heat, the blues are subtle. A pretty school of six will be less subtle.

products.title: Siamese Algae Eater (Group of 4)
products.body_html:
Siamese Algae Eater (Group of 4)

The siamensis is an active scavenger and algae eater, so four of these workers will make a nice cleaning crew.

products.title: Chinese Algae Eater (School of 4)
products.body_html:
Chinese Algae Eater (School of 4)

A group of 4 scavenging Chinese Algae Eaters will keep that fish tank clean and active.

products.title: Celestial Pearl Danio (School of 5)
products.body_html: Celestial Pearl Danio (School of 5)

products.title: Glowlight Danio (School of 5)
products.body_html:
Glowlight Danio (School of 5)

Dr. Choprae advises we keep these in schools. And five is a nice number.

products.title: White Cloud Minnow (School of 6)
products.body_html:
White Cloud Minnow (School of 6)

A school kid in China discovered this species and named it after the mountain where he found it - so why not keep these in a school?

products.title: Long Fin White Cloud Minnow (School of 6)
products.body_html:
Long Fin White Cloud Minnow (School of 6)

The carefully bred Long Fin White Cloud in a pack will be a site as the fins start to grow out as the fish mature in their school. However fish are one group of the animal kingdom that never graduates from school.

products.title: Blue King Tetra (School of 5)
products.body_html:
Blue King Tetra (School of 5)

The blue king tetra from Brazil, or simply the blue tetra, is prolific in the Brazil Amazon basin and very hardy for a wild caught fish. These tetras grow to nearly two inches and feature a pale blue body with black and white caudal (tail) fins. The color of the fins do not stand out until the fish is about one year of age. Asian imports are cheaper, but these fish tend to be washed out in color. Blue tetras from AquairumFishSale.com are imported from the Amazon basin through Belem, Brazil.

products.title: Green Fire Tetra (School of 6)
products.body_html:
Green Fire Tetra (School of 6)

The distinguishing feature of the Green Fire Tetra is the green and orange body colors. The more fish, the more green and blue body colors.

products.title: Gold Tetra (School of 6)
products.body_html:
Gold Tetra (School of 6)

Gold Tetras are caught in the wild in very large schools. These shy Tetras should be kept in groups or they tend to hide.

products.title: Daisy's Blue Eye Ricefish (School of 5)
products.body_html:
Daisy's Blue Eye Ricefish (School of 5)

In Asia, schools of Ricefish are introduced to the rice paddies to keep the population of mosquitoes in check. Do you want mosquitos in your house?

products.title: Calico Sunburst Platy (School of 4)
products.body_html:
Calico Sunburst Platy (School of 4)

The aquarium does not need a large school of these platies to get some great interaction and color.

products.title: Red Venezuelan Sailfin Cory (Gang of 3)
products.body_html: Red Venezuelan Sailfin Cory (Gang of 3)

products.title: Black Skirt Tetra (School of 6)
products.body_html:
Black Skirt Tetra (School of 6)

With their large fins and little bodies, who could resist of purchase of 6?

products.title: Red Beckford Pencilfish (School of 6)
products.body_html:
Red Beckford Pencilfish (School of 6)

Unlike writing utensils, Beckford Pencilfish are schoolers and need a few colleagues to swim with to keep a low stress level. Otherwise, they are just like pencils.

products.title: Blond Dwarf Frog (Army of 3)
products.body_html:
Blond Dwarf Frog (Army of 3)

Any group of frogs is called an army. But a little pack of blonds sounds more like a sorority.

products.title: Turquoise Danio (School of 6)
products.body_html:
Turquoise Danio (School of 6)

All danios are so active and playful in a group, it's a shame to just order a few.

products.title: Rainbow Endler - Male (Group of 6)
products.body_html:
Rainbow Endler - Male (Group of 6)

Each Rainbow Endler has a unique color pattern and a group of them will bring quite a bit of diverse color to the tank.

products.title: Albino Cherry Barb (School of 6)
products.body_html:
Albino Cherry Barb (School of 6)

Since Albino Cherry Barbs have a little cherry color and light pink, a small school of these fish will create a very colorful display. Each order comes with 6 fish.

products.title: Thai Micro Spider Crab (Consortium of 5)
products.body_html:
Thai Micro Spider Crab (Consortium of 5)

Micro crabs are small, but small or large, any group of crabs is still called a consortium.

products.title: Diamond Head Neon Tetra (School of 6)
products.body_html:
Diamond Head Neon Tetra (School of 6)

Just imagine a school of sparkling diamonds highlighted with red and blue colors swimming about. A school of these flashy diamonds might be more appealing than a diamond ring - think of the money one would save.

products.title: Blue Eye Gertrudae Rainbowfish (School of 6)
products.body_html:
Blue Eye Gertrudae Rainbowfish (School of 6)

Start these small colorful rainbows out right by introducing them into the aquarium in a school. The Gertrudae will be more likely to swim in the open waters and not hide from potential predators (hopefully there aren't any).

products.title: Bullfrog Tadpole (Army of 10)
products.body_html:
Bullfrog Tadpole (Army of 10)

A set of ten bullfrog tadpoles is a perfect pack to add to an outdoor decorative pond. And they eat mosquito larvae.

products.body_html: Red Tail Rasbora (School of 5)

products.title: Bloodfin Tetra (School of 5)
products.body_html: Bloodfin Tetra (School of 5)

products.title: Pom Pom Crab (Freshwater) (Cast of 3)
products.body_html: Pom Pom Crab (Cast of 3)

products.title: Bronze Cory (Gang of 3)
products.body_html:
Bronze Cory (Gang of 3)

A zippy little gang of three bronze cory will be easy to follow even in a well grown planted aquarium.

products.title: Long Fin Zebra Danio (School of 6)
products.body_html:
Long Fin Zebra Danio (School of 6)

Zebra Danios are one of the most playful species in the fish tank and a school of 6 will be chasing each other throughout the fish tank for hours.

products.title: Blue Wag Platy (School of 4)
products.body_html:
Blue Wag Platy (School of 4)

The black tails of the Blue Wag Platy are quite a site with four of them swimming together in the tank.

products.title: Pork Chop Rasbora (School of 6)
products.body_html:
Pork Chop Rasbora (School of 6) 

A school of Espei Rasboras flashing their bright orange and deep black colors in a tight pack make a nice display in small aquariums.

products.title: Red Hi Fin Lyre Tail Swordtail (School of 5)
products.body_html:
Red Hi-fin Lyre Tail Swordtail (School of 5)

Five of these amazing finned swordtails will be a dominant characteristic of any planted aquarium or community fish tank.

products.title: Checkered Barb (School of 5)
products.body_html:
Checkered Barb (School of 5)

The checkerboard barbs get a bright checkered pattern as they grow and interact with each other.

products.title: Black Lyre Molly (School of 4)
products.body_html:
Black Lyre Molly (School of 4)

The great aquarium fish classic Black Molly is a great school for any small to mid size aquarium.

products.title: Three Lined Pencilfish (School of 5)
products.body_html:
Three Lined Pencilfish (School of 5)

The small Three Lined Pencilfish (or Pencil Fish) is about as gentle as a fish can come - amazing that these fish survive the rough habitats of the Amazon River basin. These fish are much more at home in small schools. Three line pencils are suitable for a nano aquarium and small to midsize community fish tanks and planted aquariums.

products.title: Rainbow Shiner (School of 6)
products.body_html:
Rainbow Shiner (School of 6)

Part of the appeal, and defense, of the Shiner is the shimmer a small school makes as it moves about a body of water. The tight shimmer confuses the predator and pleases the tank owner.

products.title: Cardinal Tetra (School of 6)
products.body_html:
Cardinal Tetra (School of 6)

The most popular fish in Europe will sure to be popular in your aquarium, and these fish really need to stay in schools.

products.title: Silvertip Tetra (School of 6)
products.body_html:
Silvertip Tetra (School of 6)

Two characteristics of the Silvertip Tetra is that the color pattern gets bolder with golds and silvers as the fish matures, and these patterns are even more obvious when the fish is in a school.

products.title: Pygmy Cory (Gang of 5)
products.body_html:
Pygmy Cory (Gang of 5)

It will be much easier to spot these little guys when they are in a small school. Also, due to their diminutive size, these small Corydoras will be less stressed with colleagues if there are larger fish swimming about.

products.title: Banded Kuhli Loach (Group of 4)
products.body_html:
Banded Kuhli Loach (Group of 4)

Banded Kuhli Loaches aren't schoolers, but in the evening a small group of these Banded Kuhli Loaches will move all over the fish tank in their own individual patterns. During the day the whole pack of them will just rest.

products.title: Gold Barb (School of 6)
products.body_html:
Gold Barb (School of 6)

Barbs are always more active in a school as they interact for dominance, breeding, and just plain socializing.

products.title: Penguin Tetra (Waddle of 6)
products.body_html:
Penguin Tetra (Waddle of 6)

The more of these tetras that are put together in a school the more the group resembles a group of Antarctic penguins. And since a group of penguins is called a waddle, so is ours.

products.title: Gold White Cloud (School of 6)
products.body_html:
Gold White Cloud (School of 6) 

White clouds in nature are schooling minnows - best to keep them happy with a school?

products.title: Forktail Rainbowfish (School of 5)
products.body_html:
Forktail Rainbowfish (School of 5)

The beautiful diminutive Forktail Rainbow is very active when swimming in a school of males and females.

products.title: Gold Algae Eater (Group of 3)
products.body_html:
Gold Algae Eater (Group of 3)

Since the Gold Algae Eater will probably grow to six inches three should be enough to keep them in a social pack and not overwhelm the fish tank.

products.title: Glowlight Rasbora (School of 6)
products.body_html:
Glowlight Rasbora (School of 6) 

The Glowlight Rasbora has the same bright central markings and shape as the Harlequin Rasbora. However the Glowlight Rasbora, or Hengeli Rasbora, has a see-thru body and yellow fins. These are not as common as Harlequin Rasboras but very very hardy and peaceful. A schooling fish, these should be purchases as four or more.

products.title: Agassizi Cory (School of 3)
products.body_html:
Agassizi Cory (School of 3)

In the wild Corydoras school in the small groups of 3 to large packs of several thousand. Three buddies will be quite content to scavenge about the tank together.

products.title: Fire Red Swordtail (Group of 4)
products.body_html:
Red Swordtail (Group of 4)

A small group of male and female classic red swordtails will actively school throughout the fish tank.

products.title: Pristella Tetra (School of 6)
products.body_html:
Pristella Tetra - School of 6

X-ray Tetras make a nice school when the clear bodies and color highlights all flash around the fish tank.

products.title: Black Neon Tetra (School of 6)
products.body_html:
Black Neon Tetra (School of 6)

Like many peaceful schooling fish, black neon tetras do not do well on their own. A small group of 6 will stay in a pretty school as it moves about the aquarium.

products.title: Boesemani Rainbowfish (School of 4)
products.body_html:
Boesemani Rainbowfish (School of 4)

A group of 4 medium Boesemani ready to grow old and beautiful in the fish tank.

products.title: Candy Cane Tetra (School of 6)
products.body_html:
Candy Cane Tetra (School of 6)

Similar to Bleeding Heart Tetras with a sweeter nick name, this will be a very fancy school.

products.title: Clown Killifish (School of 4)
products.body_html:
Clown Killifish (School of 4)

Male and female Clown Killifish look similar, males have a bit more color., but they are both quite small. A small group of these small fish is the best way to view them.

products.title: Cherry Barb (School of 6)
products.body_html:
Cherry Barb (School of 6)

A nice mix of these red males and pinkish brown females will be a perfect addition to a peaceful community fish tank.

products.title: Congo Tetra (School of 5)
products.body_html:
Congo Tetra (school of 5)

A small school of the incredibly tough congo tetra will be at home in the peaceful community tank and a nice colorful African cichlid tank - oh and a planted aquarium. These are shipped medium size.

products.title: Dalmatian Lyre Molly (Pack of 4)
products.body_html:
Dalmatian Lyre Molly (Pack of 4)

A group of fish might be called a school, but the Dalmatian color of these fish are just like the Dalmatian puppy, so we had to call this little group of black and white pets a pack.

products.title: Glowlight Tetra (School of 6)
products.body_html:
Glolite Tetra (School of 6)

The long reflective lines of Glowlight Tetras (or GloLite) are made to seen in a group. Also, these skittish tetras are best kept in groups of at least 5 or 6.

products.title: Green Neon Tetra (School of 6)
products.body_html:
Green Neon Tetra (School of 6)

At maturity this wild species is smaller than the classic neon tetra, and really should be kept in a school of 6 or more.

products.title: Paleatus Cory (Gang of 3)
products.body_html:
Paleatus Cory (Gang of 3)

Active and affordable, why not keep a small group together.  3 Paleatus or Salt and Pepper Cory will be quite content.

products.title: Red Gold Honey Dwarf Gourami (School of 4)
products.body_html:
Red Gold Honey Dwarf Gourami (School of 4)

Males and females constantly interact in this group of aquarium fish, so a small school of 4 makes for a little more action with this species.

products.title: Serpae Tetra (School of 6)
products.body_html:
Serpae Tetra (School of 6)

The Red minor, or Serpae Tetra, never swims alone in the wild, and shouldn't have to in the aquarium.

products.title: Sparkling Gourami (Group of 4)
products.body_html:
Sparkling Gourami (Group of 4)

One of the few very small examples of the Gourami family, Sparkling Gourami only get to 1.5 inches at full maturity. A small pack of these is a very suitable addition to a nano aquarium or any planted fish tank.

products.title: Tiger Barb (Pride of 6)
products.body_html:
Tiger Barb (Pride of 6)

One the most popular fish in the hobby due to their adaptability and hardiness, a school (or "pride" if the were real tiger cats)  of 6 Tiger Barbs will compliment any size aquarium with almost any but the largest tank mates.

products.title: Yo Yo Loach (Group of 3)
products.body_html:
Yo Yo Loach (Group of 3)

It should be a crime, or at least a misdemeanor, to put one yo yo all by himself in a fish tank. This species lives to chase each other about the fish tank and generally just pal around.

products.title: Zebra Danio (School of 8)
products.body_html:
Zebra Danio (School of 8)

The Zebra Danio is a prolific breeder and just downright playful. Any school of these fish will add quite a bit if fun and action to any fish tank. Of course some will get eaten by larger predatory fish, so please place them with the appropriate neighbors.

products.title: Lemon Tetra (School of 6)
products.body_html:
Lemon Tetra (School of 6)

Since only the fins of this pretty schooler have the bright lemon colors, a group of 6 will provide all the yellows the aquarium needs.

products.title: Emerald Eye Rasbora (School of 6)
products.body_html:
Emerald Eye Rasbora (School of 6)

The green lamp-eyes of this Rasbora are very apparent when its swimming in a group.

products.title: Skunk Loach (Group of 3)
products.body_html:
Skunk Loach (Group of 3)

Technically a loach is a fish, and any group of fish is a school, but a "school of loaches" just doesn't have as good a ring.

products.title: Paskai Blue-eye Rainbowfish (School of 5)
products.body_html:
Paskai Rainbowfish (School of 5)

Paskai or Blue Eye Luminous Rainbowfish are small but hardy and a school of 5 will adapt well to small and mid size fish tanks.

products.title: Red Phantom Tetra (School of 6)
products.body_html:
Red Phantom Tetra (School of 6)

These bright red schooling fish are healthier in small schools.

products.body_html:
Ember Tetra (School of 6)

Smaller schooling fish like the ember tetra, also called the amandae tetra, live longer in a school than out of one.

products.title: Harlequin Rasbora (School of 6)
products.body_html:
Harlequin Rasbora (School of 6)

If Harlequins are old and alone they tend to get nippy with other peaceful fish. Keep them in a school will keep them peaceful and happy.

products.title: Indian Pea Puffer (Group of 4)
products.body_html:
Indian Pea Puffer (Group of 4)

Pea puffers are not schoolers and just one or two will be fine in a fish tank, but watching four swim about looking for little snails is much more fun than watching one.

products.title: Micro Tiger Rasbora (School of 5)
products.body_html:
Micro Tiger Rasbora (School of 5)

Every nano tank needs a pride of tigers to stalk the tank for small food flakes.

products.title: Norman's Lampeye Killifish (School of 5)
products.body_html:
Norman's Lampeye Killifish (School of 5)

The best view of these diminutive killifish is in a group where their reflective eyes look like lights continuously turning on and off.

products.title: Neon Dwarf Rainbowfish (School of 4)
products.body_html:
Neon Dwarf Rainbowfish (School of 4)

These dwarf Praecox Rainbowfish are stress free and more likely to swim about in the middle of the tank when they can swim with friends.

products.title: Platinum Halfbeak (Group of 4)
products.body_html:
Platinum Halfbeak (Group of 4)

An unusual livebearer and a top dwelling species, four half beaks will make for some lively interaction at the top level of the fish tank.

products.title: Threadfin Rainbowfish (School of 5)
products.body_html:
Threadfin Rainbowfish (School of 5)

The females with their smaller oval bodies look quite different from the males with their female attracting long fins. The interaction of a group of males and females of this species as they pair up and breakup will be lively entertainment.

products.title: White Painted Anglerfish
products.body_html:
White Painted Anglerfish: The Ghostly Hunter

The White Painted Anglerfish (Antennarius pictus), commonly known as the Painted Frogfish, is a fascinating and highly variable ambush predator prized by specialty marine aquarists. While its coloration can range from black, brown, and red to yellow, the "White" variety is particularly sought after for its striking, pale appearance, often mottled with dark spots or patches.

Like all members of the Frogfish family, this species is a master of aggressive mimicry. Its scaleless, bumpy skin and ability to change color allow it to perfectly mimic sponges or rockwork. The White Painted Anglerfish relies on its modified first dorsal spine, the illicium, which acts as a fishing rod with a worm-like lure (esca) at the tip, to draw in prey.

The Painted Anglerfish can reach a maximum size of up to 12 inches (30 cm), making it one of the larger aquarium-suitable frogfish species, although many specimens stay smaller. Due to its size and predatory nature, a minimum tank size of 50 gallons is recommended, which must contain ample structure for perching.

Diet & Compatibility:

  • Diet: This is an obligate Carnivore and an insatiable ambush predator. In the wild, it consumes small fish and crustaceans. In captivity, it must be weaned onto frozen, meaty foods, such as marine shrimp, krill, and chunks of squid, using long tongs to target-feed and simulate a live target. Avoid feeding any freshwater feeder fish as this leads to fatal nutritional deficiencies.
  • Feeding: Anglerfish are sedentary hunters with low metabolisms. They should only be fed until their stomach shows a slight bulge, typically every 2 to 4 days, depending on the size of the meal. Overfeeding is a common cause of death in captivity. It is crucial that new specimens are quickly converted from live food to a nutritious frozen diet.
  • Reef Compatibility: It is Reef Safe With Caution. The Anglerfish will not bother corals, but it will consume virtually every ornamental invertebrate (shrimp, crabs, snails) and any fish that can fit into its enormous, protusible mouth, which can swallow prey nearly twice its size. It is best suited for a fish-only (FOWLR) or species-specific aquarium.
  • Temperament: It is Semi-Aggressive / Predatory. It is passive toward humans and non-prey tankmates but is an aggressive predator. Tankmates must be large, robust, and significantly faster than the Anglerfish. They should be kept singly unless the tank is very large and the individuals are similar in size.
  • Aquarium Setup: Provide plenty of low-flow areas with live rock and structure where the fish can perch and camouflage itself. Anglerfish are known to occasionally "jet propel" themselves but primarily "walk" on their pectoral fins.

Quick Facts

  • Scientific Name: Antennarius pictus
  • Primary Common Name: White Painted Anglerfish
  • Other Common Names: Painted Frogfish, Clown Anglerfish, Spotted Frogfish
  • Origin: Indo-Pacific (Widespread)
  • Max Size: Up to 12 inches (30 cm)
  • Temperament: Semi-Aggressive / Predatory
  • Diet: Carnivore (Must be trained to frozen meaty foods)
  • Minimum Tank Size: 50+ Gallons
  • Reef Compatibility: With Caution (Will eat small fish and all ornamental shrimp/crabs)

products.title: Red Anglerfish
products.body_html:
Red Wartskin Anglerfish: The Master of Camouflage

The Red Wartskin Anglerfish (Antennarius maculatus), often referred to as a Frogfish, is a captivating and highly effective ambush predator popular in the marine hobby. The "Red" variation showcases vibrant hues ranging from orange and red to pink, often mottled with darker spots or saddles, allowing it to seamlessly blend into red sponges and rockwork.

This species is a master of camouflage, possessing a textured, wart-like skin and the remarkable ability to change its coloration to match its surroundings. Instead of swimming, the Anglerfish uses its modified pectoral and pelvic fins to "walk" or hop across the substrate. Its primary hunting tool is the illicium (a modified dorsal spine) which acts as a fishing rod, tipped with a fleshy lure (the esca) used to attract unsuspecting prey.

Reaching an average maximum size of only 4 to 6 inches (10-15 cm), the Wartskin Anglerfish is one of the smaller, more manageable Frogfish species. Despite its size, it possesses a massive, protusible mouth capable of consuming fish and crustaceans nearly its own size. It requires a minimum aquarium of 30 gallons with ample live rock for perching and hunting.

Diet & Compatibility:

  • Diet: This is an obligate Carnivore and a highly effective predator. Its diet in the wild consists of small fish and shrimp. In captivity, it must be trained to accept non-living, meaty foods like frozen krill, chunks of squid, and marine shrimp, often requiring target feeding using tongs to mimic movement. Never feed freshwater feeder fish.
  • Feeding: Due to their sedentary nature, Anglerfish have a low metabolism and should be fed sparingly, typically every 2 to 3 days. Overfeeding can be a significant health risk. It may initially require live food (like feeder shrimp) to initiate feeding, but conversion to frozen food is essential for long-term health.
  • Reef Compatibility: It is generally considered Reef Safe With Caution. While it will ignore corals, anemones, and large mobile invertebrates (like conchs or snails), it will systematically hunt and consume any ornamental shrimp, crabs, and fish that can fit into its mouth. It is best housed in a dedicated fish-only (FOWLR) tank or a species-specific setup.
  • Temperament: It is Semi-Aggressive / Predatory. It is peaceful toward humans and very large tankmates, but extremely predatory toward small fish and invertebrates. Tankmates must be large, robust, and fast-moving. It should be kept singly unless the aquarium is very large and the specimens are introduced together.
  • Aquarium Setup: Provide low to moderate flow and plenty of crevices, ledges, and sponges for perching and camouflage. Since they are inactive swimmers, they require less open water but benefit greatly from a stable environment and excellent water quality.

Quick Facts

  • Scientific Name: Antennarius maculatus
  • Primary Common Name: Red Wartskin Anglerfish
  • Other Common Names: Wartskin Frogfish, Clown Angler, Spotted Anglerfish
  • Origin: Indo-Pacific (Widespread)
  • Max Size: 4-6 inches (10-15 cm)
  • Temperament: Semi-Aggressive / Predatory
  • Diet: Carnivore (Must be trained to frozen meaty foods)
  • Minimum Tank Size: 30+ Gallons
  • Reef Compatibility: With Caution (Will eat small fish and all ornamental shrimp/crabs)

products.title: Blind Cave Tetra
products.body_html:
Blind Cave Tetra

The exotic blind cave fish were discovered in a completely dark cave in Mexico. Also known as a Mexican Tetra, Blind Cave Tetra or Blind Fish, these fish don't have eyes or pigmentation. This unusual looking fish is a bit nippy as it tends to overcompensate, but generally fits well in community tank as long as there's good room to swim. These fish are very active as they troll for food and explore their surroundings - interesting additions to the fish tank.

  • Scientific Name: Astyanax Mexicanus
  • Origin: Mexico, Central America
  • Lifespan: 4 years
  • Max Size: 3 inches
  • Food: flake, live, frozen
  • Shipping Size: Approx. 1 1/2 inch

products.title: Scarlet Reef Hermit Crab
products.body_html:
Scarlet Reef Hermit Crab: The Vibrant Algae Cleaner

The Scarlet Reef Hermit Crab (Paguristes cadenati) is one of the most popular, colorful, and beneficial members of the marine aquarium clean-up crew. This species is immediately recognizable by its bright red legs and claws, which contrast beautifully with its yellow or yellowish-orange eyestalks and a relatively small, orange-red body.

Native to the Caribbean and Western Atlantic, the Scarlet Reef Hermit Crab is highly valued by hobbyists for its voracious appetite for nuisance algae. Unlike some other hermit crabs, which can become aggressive toward tankmates, this species is generally peaceful and is less likely to prey on snails for their shells, provided ample empty shells are available.

Reaching a maximum body size (excluding the shell) of about 1.5 inches (3.8 cm), this crab is suitable for tanks of almost any size and is a hardy, long-lived invertebrate that works tirelessly to maintain a clean environment.

Diet & Compatibility:

  • Diet: The Scarlet Reef Hermit Crab is an Omnivore / Scavenger. It is primarily a consumer of algae and detritus. It is particularly effective at grazing on green hair algae (filamentous algae) and is known to consume cyanobacteria (red slime algae).
  • Feeding: In a well-established reef with sufficient algae and detritus, supplemental feeding may not be necessary. If algae is scarce, supplement its diet with dried seaweed (Nori), algae wafers, marine pellets, or small pieces of meaty foods like mysis shrimp or brine shrimp.
  • Shell Supply: Like all hermit crabs, it requires an ample supply of empty shells of various sizes (with wide, circular openings) to move into as it grows. Failing to provide this will increase the risk of it attacking and killing snails for their shells.
  • Reef Compatibility: It is considered Reef Safe. It will not harm corals, although it will walk over them while scavenging. Its relatively peaceful nature makes it one of the best hermit crab choices for a mixed reef tank.
  • Temperament: It is Peaceful / Semi-Aggressive (toward other hermits). It is generally peaceful toward fish and other invertebrates, but multiple hermits may compete for shells. It should not be housed with large, aggressive fish (like puffers or triggerfish) that may view it as food.
  • Clean-up Role: This crab is an excellent clean-up crew member, consuming nuisance algae and helping to aerate the sand bed as it forages.

Quick Facts

  • Scientific Name: Paguristes cadenati
  • Primary Common Name: Scarlet Reef Hermit Crab
  • Other Common Names: Red Reef Hermit Crab, Red Leg Hermit Crab, Scarlet Hermit
  • Origin: Western Atlantic (Caribbean, Gulf of Mexico)
  • Max Size (Body): ~1.5 inches (3.8 cm)
  • Temperament: Peaceful / Semi-Aggressive (shell competition)
  • Diet: Omnivore / Scavenger (Excellent algae and detritus eater)
  • Minimum Tank Size: 10 Gallons
  • Reef Compatibility: Reef Safe (Must provide extra shells)
  • Care Level: Easy

products.title: Haitian Reef Anemone
products.body_html:
Haitian Reef Anemone: The Aggressive Condy
